original_abstract: What are the defining characteristics of the individuals behind science’s most significant breakthroughs? This study addresses this question by examining a comprehensive dataset of over 750 major discoveries, encompassing all Nobel Prize winners as well as prominent non-Nobel achievers. By analyzing their demographic, institutional, and economic backgrounds, we establish a general profile of the modern scientific discoverer. Our findings reveal a significant shift toward interdisciplinarity: 54% of Nobel Prize winners and 42% of major non-Nobel discoverers held degrees in two or more different academic fields at the time of their achievement. This suggests that broad methodological training is a critical driver of innovation. Furthermore, science is becoming increasingly concentrated in elite institutions, with the top 25 global universities accounting for 30% of all major discoveries. We also observe a notable aging trend; breakthroughs by scientists under 32 have plummeted, while the "golden age" for discovery now sits between 35 and 45. Researchers over 60 contribute to only 1–3% of clinical or theoretical milestones. Additionally, we document an increasing lag between the time of discovery and the awarding of the Nobel Prize, highlighting institutional delays in recognizing breakthroughs. Beyond career and age, we assess the influence of geography, gender, and religion, noting a persistent gender gap and a migration of talent toward North America. These results suggest that to accelerate scientific progress, funding agencies and universities should move away from incentivizing narrow specialization and instead foster interdisciplinary environments that encourage the integration of diverse methodologies.
humanized_abstract: Can we characterise the attributes of the people who make the major discoveries in science? The study behind this article analysed a dataset of 765 major scientific discoveries, including all Nobel Prizes and other major scientific breakthroughs that were recognised as being of similar importance. The researchers then compared the demographic, institutional and economic characteristics of the discoverers. They found that 54% of Nobel Prize winners and 42% of other major discoverers had degrees in two different fields of science, indicating a need for more general training across disciplines to enable interdisciplinary discoveries. They also found that the largest scientific institutions in the world were responsible for 30% of the discoveries in the dataset, and that there had been a fall in the number of discoveries made by scientists under 32. The researchers call the new "golden years" of discovery between 35 and 45, and note that scientists over 60 make up contributions to less than 1-3% of all clinical and theoretical discoveries. They also note a lag time between discovery and when the Nobel Prize is awarded, which they say can last up to 15 years. They also explored the effects of geography, gender and religion on scientific discoveries and found that a gender bias for women scientists continues, and that the best scientists in the world are also migrating to America. The study's findings suggest that for the progress of science, research grants should be given to broadly-based fields of study and that universities should not encourage the fragmentation of science into separate faculties, but encourage instead an interdisciplinary approach by combining faculties of science.
